ops.text:
## Lane: Rig + item 1 (concurrency sweep) — EARLY RETURN (two shape changes)

Cap $12. Actual spend $3.03 (4 cost calibrations only; the specified sweep was NOT run — see blockers).

### Rig built + validated (reusable) at ~/.cc-steer/experiments/WFA/scripts/
- `shim/claude` — PATH-first capture shim. spawnllm (cc_transcript.judge.llm -> spawnllm.extract) invokes `claude -p --json-schema ... --output-format json` and DISCARDS the cost envelope; `extract()` returns only the parsed model. The shim sits first on PATH: real claude writes its full envelope to a private regular file (synchronous, no 64KiB Node truncation), the shim keeps it for accounting, then `cat`s it to fd1 (spawnllm's own regular-file stdout target). Fixed a real bug: BSD mktemp only expands TRAILING X's, so `env.XXXX.json` collided under concurrency — now `env-XXXXXXXXXX` (verified 30 parallel calls -> 30 unique files).
- `run_stage.py` — fresh `cp -c` clone per run, clears capture dir, times the real `cc-steer <stage> --db <clone> --model <tier> --concurrency N --limit M --no-sync` subprocess, parses judged/refined report + every captured envelope (total_cost_usd + usage tokens). Writes results/<name>.json.
- `run_stage.sh` — wrapper: `eval "$(ccp env)"` spend routing (routes via CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR; shim forwards env so routing is preserved), PATH shim, `uv run --no-sync`.
- `make_slices.py` — persists slices.json (fixed slice = first-N-by-id; unjudged/unrefined both ORDER BY e.id, deterministic on byte-identical clones, so `--limit N` reproduces it).
- `table.py` — renders results/*.json into the results table.

### Per-stage / per-tier cost table (the robust deliverable; ~35K-token context per row dominates)
```
 stage    tier   conc  wall_s  ok  fail  rows/s      usd   usd/row   tok/row
 triage   large     5   22.28   5    0    0.224  1.85877  0.371753   35241.6   (opus)
 triage  medium     5   20.85   5    0    0.240  0.35360  0.070720   35862.2   (sonnet; cache_read 134K -> lower)
 triage   small     5   58.73   5    0    0.085  0.29953  0.059906   24713.4   (haiku; verbose out, 3x slower)
 refine   large     3   18.51   3    0    0.162  0.51556  0.171855   21314.7   (opus)
```
usd/row: triage opus $0.372, sonnet $0.071, haiku $0.060; refine opus $0.172. Rows carry a full hydrated transcript window (~21-36K tokens), so cost is context-bound, not prompt-bound — cheaper tiers don't shrink token count.

### BLOCKER 1 — budget: the specified sweep is 20-25x the cap
Full spec = 3 triage runs x 200 rows (600) + 2 refine x 100 (200). At production `large`/opus: triage 600 x $0.372 = $223 + refine 200 x $0.172 = $34 = ~$257. All-sonnet ~$54, all-haiku ~$48. NO configuration of the full 200/100 slice x the sweep fits $12. (Pipeline production tier is `large` per pipeline.py run_triage/run_refine tier="large".)

### BLOCKER 2 — non-stationary workspace + shared rate limits (measurement invalid right now)
cc-steer is an EDITABLE install; the workspace source is being rewritten mid-session by sibling lanes. Observed: triage PROMPT_VERSION 6->7, refine 3->4, and cc_steer/triage.py+refine.py mtimes 09:23-09:24 (during my calibrations). The triage unjudged set jumped 306 (v6) -> 5776 (v7), so slices.json (v6/v3) is already stale and cal-triage (v6) is not comparable to a run now (v7). `jj op log` shows wfs@ and n3@ active on the same repo -> they also consume the same account's rate budget, so any 429/rate-limit-inflection measurement is confounded and non-attributable. A clean concurrency sweep requires: (a) frozen code, (b) this lane the SOLE LLM consumer.

### Preliminary concurrency inference (unmeasured, to confirm on a clean sweep)
At ~35K tok/row and ~20s/call, in-flight token demand scales ~C x 35K: C=8 ~280K, C=24 ~840K per call-window. With opus TPM caps this predicts 16/24 mostly buys 429 exposure, not throughput, once TPM-bound. So DEFAULT_CONCURRENCY=8 is a plausible keep; 16/24 is where inflection likely appears. NOT a measured recommendation.

Artifacts: ~/.cc-steer/experiments/WFA/{slices.json, results/*.json, results/TABLE.txt, scripts/}.
